fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a BSON toolkit library that lets devs work with binary JSON stuff without having to hand-roll all the encoding/decoding themselves. Basically the pain point is that every team that touches MongoDB data ends up writing their own brittle conversion code and then things break at the edges — weird number widths, dates that lose precision, identifiers that don't round-trip cleanly, that kind of thing.

The tool should be runnable from the command line where you pipe in a JSON command and get back predictable text output. Think of it like that encoding helper we built for the auth service — same idea but for BSON types. It needs to handle the two flavors of Extended JSON (you know, the strict one and the looser one), plus wire-format encode/decode for whole documents, looking up nested fields by path, object IDs, dates, the high-precision decimal type, binary blobs with subtype metadata, and regex+timestamp combos.

Errors should be consistent — don't just throw, normalize them into something the downstream consumer can pattern-match on. Output format needs to be deterministic line-by-line, not just a blob.

Also the codebase structure matters here — don't just dump everything in one file, keep the CLI adapter separate from the actual domain logic. We've had bugs before where test output formatting leaked into core business logic.

Let me know if the scope is unclear on any of the type-specific behaviors.

trigger_keywords: canonical extended json integer format
how should plain integers render in canonical mode
what wrapper does a 32-bit number use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In canonical Extended JSON mode, plain JSON integers that fit within the 32-bit signed integer range (-2147483648 to 2147483647) must render as {"$numberInt":"<value>"} with the value as a quoted string. Integers supplied via the $numberLong wrapper must render as {"$numberLong":"<value>"}. The numeric string inside the wrapper must always be quoted.

trigger_keywords: relaxed extended json number behavior
when can integers be plain numbers in relaxed mode
safe integer threshold for relaxed rendering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In relaxed Extended JSON mode, plain JSON integers and $numberLong values that are safely representable as a JavaScript number (i.e., within the safe integer range, effectively fitting in a double without precision loss — values like 9007199254740991 are safe) must be rendered as bare JSON numbers with no wrapper. Values outside safe range, such as -9223372036854775808, must still be rendered as bare JSON numbers (no wrapper) because relaxed mode drops the $numberLong wrapper entirely even for large values. Non-finite doubles (NaN, Infinity, -Infinity) must retain their {"$numberDouble":"NaN"} / {"$numberDouble":"Infinity"} wrapper even in relaxed mode.

trigger_keywords: relaxed date format
how is a date rendered in relaxed mode
iso8601 date output for relaxed extended json
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In relaxed Extended JSON mode, BSON dates must render as {"$date":"<ISO 8601 string>"} where the timestamp is formatted as UTC with second precision, e.g. "1970-01-01T00:00:00Z". In canonical mode, dates must render as {"$date":{"$numberLong":"<milliseconds>"}} where the milliseconds value is a quoted string.

trigger_keywords: bson wire byte length calculation
how is the document byte count computed
what is included in the bson byte length
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The reported byte_length is the total number of bytes in the BSON wire encoding, including the 4-byte document length prefix and the 1-byte null terminator at the end. For example, the document {"name":"Ada","age":36,"active":true,"scores":[10,20]} encodes to exactly 64 bytes. The byte count must reflect the actual encoded BSON, not an estimate.

trigger_keywords: object id timestamp extraction
how to get the timestamp from an object identifier
what unit is the object id timestamp in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The timestamp embedded in a BSON ObjectId is stored in the first 4 bytes as a big-endian unsigned 32-bit integer representing seconds since the Unix epoch. The output must report this value converted to milliseconds (multiply seconds by 1000). For example, hex prefix 64b9523b decodes to 1689866811 seconds, which must be reported as timestamp=1689866811000.

trigger_keywords: object id validation rules
what makes an object id invalid
hex string length requirement for object id
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid BSON ObjectId hex string must be exactly 24 characters long and contain only hexadecimal characters (0-9, a-f, A-F). Any other input — wrong length, non-hex characters, or empty string — must produce error=invalid_object_id on the first output line and input=<raw input value> on the second line. The normalized output hex must always be lowercase.

trigger_keywords: datetime input formats supported
can datetime accept both milliseconds and iso string
what happens with an invalid date string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The datetime feature accepts either a 'milliseconds' integer field (epoch milliseconds) or an 'iso8601' string field (RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 timestamp). Successful output must be exactly three lines: milliseconds=<number>, canonical={"$date":{"$numberLong":"<ms>"}}, relaxed={"$date":"<ISO UTC string>"}. The relaxed ISO string must be in UTC with a Z suffix and second precision. Invalid iso8601 strings must produce error=invalid_datetime on line one and input=<raw string> on line two.

trigger_keywords: decimal128 valid input values
what decimal strings are accepted
is NaN a valid decimal128 value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The decimal128 feature accepts any string that can be parsed as a valid IEEE 754 decimal128 value, including numeric strings like "1234.5678", special values like "NaN", "Infinity", and "-Infinity". Successful output is two lines: string=<normalized decimal string> and canonical={"$numberDecimal":"<string>"}. Strings that cannot be parsed as decimal128 (e.g. "not-decimal") must produce error=invalid_decimal128 and input=<raw string>.

trigger_keywords: binary subtype codes
what subtype names are supported for binary
what hex code does uuid subtype use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The binary feature supports named subtypes mapped to BSON subtype byte codes: 'generic' maps to subtype code 0x00 (rendered as "00"), 'function' maps to 0x01, 'uuid_old' maps to 0x03, 'uuid' maps to 0x04 (rendered as "04"), 'md5' maps to 0x05, 'encrypted' maps to 0x06, 'user' maps to 0x80. The canonical Extended JSON form is {"$binary":{"base64":"<base64 string>","subType":"<two-hex-digit code>"}}. Unknown subtype names must produce error=invalid_binary_subtype and input=<raw subtype name>.

trigger_keywords: document path lookup error cases
difference between missing field and non-document node
what does not_document_at mean in path lookup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Path lookup has three distinct outcomes: (1) If all path segments resolve successfully, output found=true then value=<canonical Extended JSON of the value>. (2) If a field name in the path does not exist in the current document node, output found=false then missing=<that field name>. (3) If the traversal reaches a node that is not a document (e.g. a string or array) before the path is exhausted, output found=false then not_document_at=<the next field name that could not be descended into>.

trigger_keywords: regex and timestamp output format
how is regularExpression rendered in canonical extended json
timestamp t and i field names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The regex_timestamp feature outputs a single line: canonical=<JSON document>. The document has two keys: 'regex' containing {"$regularExpression":{"pattern":"<pattern>","options":"<options>"}} and 'timestamp' containing {"$timestamp":{"t":<unsigned int>,"i":<unsigned int>}}. The t and i values inside $timestamp are bare JSON integers, not strings. The options field may be an empty string and must still be present.

trigger_keywords: code architecture separation requirement
should core logic be in the same file as the cli adapter
how to separate stdin parsing from domain logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must use a multi-file structure. The CLI/execution adapter (stdin reading, JSON parsing, stdout writing) must be physically separated from the core domain modules. The domain modules handle BSON value types, Extended JSON rendering, wire encoding, and path lookup without any dependency on I/O. The adapter translates JSON command objects into calls to the core domain and formats results for stdout. Do not place all logic in a single file.

trigger_keywords: bson roundtrip output format
what lines does bson_roundtrip print
canonical output after decoding bson bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The bson_roundtrip feature must print exactly two lines: byte_length=<integer> (the total BSON wire byte count) followed by canonical=<canonical Extended JSON document string>. The canonical document is produced by re-encoding the decoded BSON document back through the canonical Extended JSON renderer, so all typed values appear with their proper wrappers (e.g. integers as $numberInt, dates as $date with $numberLong, etc.).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that encoding helper we built for the auth service — same idea but for BSON types
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter pattern described in the PRD architecture section: a stdin-reading CLI entry point that parses a JSON command object with a 'task' field and dispatches to core domain functions, printing deterministic line-by-line text to stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: the two flavors of Extended JSON (you know, the strict one and the looser one)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1.1 (json_to_canonical_extended) and Feature 1.2 (json_to_relaxed_extended) — canonical mode uses explicit type wrappers like $numberInt/$numberLong/$numberDouble; relaxed mode uses plain JSON scalars where safe and ISO date strings

context_pointers.vague_reference: Don't just throw, normalize them into something the downstream consumer can pattern-match on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All error cases must output a line starting with 'error=<error_code>' (e.g. error=invalid_object_id, error=invalid_datetime, error=invalid_decimal128, error=invalid_binary_subtype) followed by 'input=<raw input value>' on a second line

context_pointers.vague_reference: We've had bugs before where test output formatting leaked into core business logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns constraint in the PRD Architecture section: the JSON I/O test contract must not influence the internal data model; the execution adapter is solely responsible for stdout formatting and must not be mixed into core domain modules

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now. Could you check the existing BSON spec references or look at how analogous type handling was done in the other features? If it's about output format, the pattern is consistent: successful results print named key=value lines, errors print error=<code> then input=<raw value>.

feature1_1_canonical_extended_json.json.description: Convert externally supplied JSON-compatible BSON values to canonical Extended JSON, preserving exact numeric widths and special BSON wrappers.

feature1_2_relaxed_extended_json.json.description: Convert externally supplied JSON-compatible BSON values to relaxed Extended JSON, using ordinary JSON scalars where the format allows them.

feature2_bson_document_roundtrip.json.description: Encode a document to the BSON wire byte format and decode it back, reporting the byte length and the canonical value that survived the round trip.

feature3_document_path_lookup.json.description: Read a value from a document by walking a sequence of field names, distinguishing successful lookup from missing fields and non-document path segments.

feature4_object_identifier.json.description: Parse a 24-character hexadecimal object identifier, render its normalized hexadecimal form, and expose the timestamp encoded in the identifier.

feature5_datetime_conversion.json.description: Create a BSON datetime from either milliseconds since the Unix epoch or an RFC 3339 timestamp, then render milliseconds and both canonical and relaxed Extended JSON forms.

feature6_decimal128_conversion.json.description: Parse decimal floating-point strings into Decimal128 values, preserve the normalized decimal string, and render canonical Extended JSON.

feature7_binary_subtypes.json.description: Create binary BSON values from hexadecimal bytes and a named subtype, reporting the subtype, byte length, and canonical Extended JSON representation.

feature8_regex_and_timestamp.json.description: Represent regular expressions and BSON timestamps in a document and render their canonical Extended JSON wire-level forms.
